items.html:
What communication is

Communication is how we share information, thoughts and feelings with other people. Every time you speak, write, gesture, smile or simply listen, you are communicating. In care work it happens constantly — when you greet someone at the start of a shift, explain what you are about to do, or notice that a person seems quieter than usual.

Good communication is a two-way process. It is not only about giving information; it is about making sure the other person has understood, and that you have understood them. It involves words, but also tone of voice, expression and body language. When you get it right, the people you support feel heard, respected and safe.

Communication also flows in both directions at once. While you are speaking, the person is telling you things through their words, their face and their behaviour, and your task is to receive those messages as carefully as you send your own.

Why it is central to good care

Communication sits at the heart of everything you do. It is how you build trusting relationships, find out what someone wants, and give care that fits the individual rather than a routine. Poor communication is one of the most common causes of mistakes, complaints and distress, and it can leave people feeling ignored or unsafe.

  • Safety: clear handovers and records mean nothing important is missed.
  • Dignity: explaining and asking, rather than assuming, protects a person's rights.
  • Wellbeing: a friendly, patient manner reduces anxiety and loneliness.
  • Teamwork: sharing clear information helps colleagues and families work together.

Throughout this course you will build practical skills to communicate well with every person you support.

The communication cycle

Communication works as a cycle. A sender has a message they want to share. They choose how to send it — spoken words, writing, a gesture or a picture. The receiver takes in that message and makes sense of it. They then give feedback, such as a nod, a reply or a puzzled look, which tells the sender whether the message got through.

Feedback is what turns one-way talking into real communication. It lets you check understanding and adjust as you go. If a person looks confused, you can slow down, rephrase, or try a different method rather than carrying on regardless. Without feedback you are only guessing whether you have been understood.

How it breaks down

At any point the cycle can fail. The sender may be unclear or use jargon. The message may be lost in a noisy room or a rushed handover. The receiver may mishear, be distracted, or understand the words differently from how they were meant. Even a small gap at one stage can change the whole meaning.

  • Check, do not assume: ask the person to tell you what they have understood.
  • Watch for feedback: a frown or silence is information, not rudeness.
  • Close the loop: repeat back key details to confirm you have them right.

Taking a few seconds to confirm understanding prevents most misunderstandings before they cause harm.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete this description of the communication cycle.
items.textField: The *sender* has a message and chooses how to share it. The *receiver* takes it in and makes sense of it, then gives *feedback* so the sender can *check* that the message was understood.

items.html:
Verbal communication

Verbal communication is the words you use, spoken or written. In care the way you say something often matters as much as the words themselves. A warm, calm tone reassures; a rushed or sharp tone can worry someone even when your words are kind.

Choose plain, respectful language. Avoid jargon, abbreviations and baby talk. Speak clearly and at a steady pace, give the person time to answer, and use their preferred name. Pitch, volume and pace all carry meaning, so stay aware of how you sound, not just what you say. Written notes and messages count as verbal communication too, so keep them clear and easy to read.

Non-verbal communication

Much of what we communicate is non-verbal — without words. Facial expression, eye contact, posture, gestures, touch and personal space all send messages, and they are often believed more readily than the words themselves. People with communication difficulties may rely on these signals heavily, both to understand you and to express themselves.

  • Body language: an open, relaxed posture invites conversation.
  • Facial expression: a genuine smile puts people at ease.
  • Eye contact: shows you are listening, but respect cultural differences.
  • Match your signals: your words and body language should agree.

Watch the other person's non-verbal cues too — they often reveal how someone truly feels, especially when their words and their body language do not seem to match. A person who says they are fine while looking tense may need you to gently check in.

items.question: You are helping someone who seems anxious. Which approach uses non-verbal communication well?

answers.text: Sit at their level, keep a relaxed posture and a warm expression, and give them time.
answers.feedback: Correct. Matching calm body language to your reassuring words helps the person feel safe.

answers.text: Stand over them and speak quickly so the task is finished sooner.
answers.feedback: Standing over someone and rushing can feel intimidating and increase their anxiety.

answers.text: Avoid all eye contact so you do not intrude on them.
answers.feedback: Some eye contact shows you are listening; avoiding it completely can feel cold or dismissive.

answers.text: Fold your arms and glance at the clock while they speak.
answers.feedback: Closed posture and clock-watching signal impatience and discourage the person from talking.

items.html:
What active listening is

Listening is more than staying quiet while someone speaks. Active listening means giving the person your full attention and showing that you are taking in what they say. It tells the person that they matter and helps you understand what they really mean, not just the words on the surface.

To listen actively, face the person, make comfortable eye contact and put distractions aside. Do not interrupt or finish their sentences. Let there be pauses; silence gives people time to think, especially if they find talking difficult or tiring.

Active listening takes real effort and concentration. It is easy to drift off, plan your reply or worry about your next task, but the person will sense it if your attention wanders.

Showing you have understood

Good listeners check that they have understood correctly. Small responses keep the conversation going and prove you are engaged without taking over.

  • Reflect: repeat back key points in your own words.
  • Clarify: ask gentle questions if something is unclear.
  • Encourage: nod, or say a quiet yes, to show you are following.
  • Notice feelings: listen for the emotion behind the words.

Open questions that begin with what, how or tell me draw out more than questions with a simple yes or no answer. Resist the urge to jump in with advice; sometimes a person just needs to be heard.

items.question: Which of these is the best example of active listening?

answers.text: Facing the person, letting them finish, then reflecting back what you heard to check you understood.
answers.feedback: Correct. Full attention plus reflecting back shows respect and confirms understanding.

answers.text: Tidying the room while they talk so you do not waste any time.
answers.feedback: Doing other tasks signals the person is not your priority and means you will miss cues.

answers.text: Finishing their sentences to move the conversation along.
answers.feedback: Finishing sentences can put words in their mouth and stops them expressing themselves.

answers.text: Nodding along while quietly planning your reply.
answers.feedback: Planning your response means you are not truly taking in what is being said.

items.html:
What gets in the way

Even with the best intentions, many things can block communication. Recognising barriers is the first step to removing them. Barriers can come from the surroundings, from a person's health, or from feelings and circumstances.

  • Environmental: noise, poor lighting, no privacy or a busy space.
  • Physical and sensory: hearing or sight loss, pain, tiredness or the effects of a stroke.
  • Language: a different first language, jargon, or unfamiliar words.
  • Emotional: anxiety, low mood, embarrassment or feeling rushed.

Often several barriers combine at once. A tired person in a noisy day room, who is also in some pain, faces three obstacles at the same time, and each one makes the others harder to overcome.

Why barriers matter

When a barrier is present, messages get missed or misread. A person with hearing loss may nod politely without catching a word. Someone in pain or distress may not take in your explanation. A jargon-filled sentence can leave a person too embarrassed to admit they did not understand, so they simply agree.

Barriers are not the person's fault, and they are rarely permanent. Your job is to spot them and adapt. Notice when someone seems to be struggling, ask yourself what might be getting in the way, and change the environment or your approach. A quiet, unhurried, well-lit space with your full attention removes many barriers before they start.

items.question: A noisy, poorly lit room is an example of an environmental barrier to communication.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Noise, poor lighting and lack of privacy are all environmental barriers that you can often reduce.
items.wrongMsg: This is actually true. Noise and poor lighting are environmental barriers — changing the setting can remove them.

items.html:
Adapting the setting and yourself

Once you notice a barrier, small changes make a big difference. Start with the environment: reduce background noise, turn off the television, improve the lighting and find a private space where the person feels comfortable to talk.

Then adapt yourself. Face the person so they can see your lips and expression, speak clearly without shouting, and use short sentences. Give the person time and do not rush. Check that hearing aids are switched on and that glasses are clean and to hand. These simple steps solve many everyday difficulties before you try anything else.

Stay calm and unhurried in your own manner. If you seem stressed or in a rush, the person is likely to feel it and become more anxious, which makes communication harder still.

Finding the right method

If talking alone is not working, try another way to get the message across, and be willing to combine several.

  • Show and demonstrate: point, gesture or show an object.
  • Use aids: pictures, easy-read leaflets or writing things down.
  • Get support: a professional interpreter for a different language.
  • Check understanding: ask the person to tell you what they will do next.

Always be patient and never pretend to understand when you do not. If you cannot make yourself understood, ask a colleague for help and record what worked so the whole team can use it.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete these tips for overcoming barriers.
items.textField: To help someone with hearing loss, reduce background *noise*, make sure their *hearing* aid is switched on, *face* the person so they can see your lips, and *check* that they have understood.

items.html:
Everyone is different

No two people communicate in exactly the same way. A person's needs, wishes and preferences are shaped by their health, culture, background, language and personality. Part of person-centred care is finding out how each individual likes to communicate, rather than treating everyone the same.

Under the Accessible Information Standard, services must ask about, record and meet the communication and information needs of the people they support. This means knowing, for example, if someone needs large print, an interpreter, or information explained slowly, and making sure that support is actually in place when they need it.

Preferences cover more than method. They include the language a person uses, the name they like to be called, the pace that suits them, and who they are happy for you to share information with.

How to find out

You learn a person's preferences in several ways, and you keep learning as you get to know them over time.

  • Ask the person: they are the expert on their own needs.
  • Read the care plan: it should record their communication needs.
  • Ask those who know them: family, friends and other staff.
  • Observe: notice what helps and what causes frustration.

Record what you find so the whole team can respond consistently. Preferences can change — after an illness, or as a condition progresses — so review them regularly rather than assuming they stay the same.

items.question: Who is the best first source of information about how a person prefers to communicate?

answers.text: The person themselves, as they are the expert on their own needs and wishes.
answers.feedback: Correct. Always start with the person; they know their own preferences best.

answers.text: Whichever method happens to be quickest for the staff team.
answers.feedback: Care must fit the person, not staff convenience; this ignores their wishes.

answers.text: A general assumption based on the person's age.
answers.feedback: Assumptions based on age or diagnosis are unreliable and can be disrespectful.

answers.text: Only the written care plan, without speaking to the person.
answers.feedback: The care plan is useful, but it should not replace asking and observing the person directly.

items.html:
Aids that support communication

Many people use aids to help them send or receive messages. Knowing what is available means you can support each person to communicate as fully as possible.

  • Hearing aids and loop systems: help people with hearing loss.
  • Glasses and good lighting: support people with sight loss.
  • Picture boards and symbols: let people point to what they need.
  • Makaton: signs and symbols used alongside speech.
  • Easy-read: simple words with supporting pictures.

You do not need to be an expert in every system. What matters is knowing which aids each person you support relies on, and making sure they can always reach and use them.

Language and technology

When someone's first language is not English, use a professional interpreter or an approved translation service rather than relying on family, who may not translate accurately or may find the topic difficult. For deaf people who use British Sign Language, a qualified BSL interpreter may be needed.

Technology offers more options every year: text-to-speech apps, tablets with symbol software, video-relay interpreting and volume-amplified phones. Whatever the aid, look after it well — keep hearing aids charged and clean, make sure boards are within reach, and check equipment is working. An aid only helps if it is available and in good order when the person needs it. If you notice something is broken, running low on batteries or missing, report it promptly so it can be fixed or replaced.

items.question: A person you support speaks little English and needs to discuss their care. What is the best approach?

answers.text: Arrange a professional interpreter or an approved translation service.
answers.feedback: Correct. A trained interpreter gives accurate, confidential and impartial communication.

answers.text: Ask their young child to translate the conversation for you.
answers.feedback: Relying on family, especially a child, risks errors and places an unfair burden on them.

answers.text: Speak louder in English until they understand you.
answers.feedback: Volume does not overcome a language barrier and can feel disrespectful.

answers.text: Avoid the conversation until someone bilingual happens to be on shift.
answers.feedback: Delaying important discussions denies the person a say in their own care.

items.html:
Sensory loss

Sight or hearing loss changes how a person takes in information, so adapt to their remaining senses. For someone with hearing loss, face them in good light, reduce noise, speak clearly and be ready to write things down. For someone with sight loss, say who you are as you approach, describe what is happening, and explain before you touch them or move anything.

Some people have both hearing and sight loss. They may use touch, objects of reference or a communicator guide. Always find out the individual's preferred method rather than guessing, and give them extra time, because taking in information through one sense takes longer.

Dementia and learning disability

People living with dementia may struggle to find words or follow long sentences. Keep language simple, ask one thing at a time, and allow plenty of time to respond. Non-verbal warmth — a calm tone, a smile, gentle body language — reassures when words are hard to follow.

  • Stay patient: never rush, correct harshly or argue over details.
  • Use what works: pictures, objects, gestures and familiar routines.
  • Meet them where they are: respond to feelings, not just facts.

For a person with a learning disability, easy-read information, Makaton and picture boards can all support understanding and choice. Check what they have understood by asking them to show or tell you, rather than assuming a nod means yes.

items.question: When supporting a person living with dementia, it is best to give several instructions at once to save time.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Give one simple instruction at a time and allow plenty of time to respond; several at once can overwhelm.
items.wrongMsg: This is false. Several instructions at once can overwhelm; give one simple step at a time with time to respond.

items.html:
Communicating within the team

You rarely work alone. Care depends on clear communication between you, your colleagues, families and other professionals such as nurses, GPs and social workers. Good teamwork keeps everyone working to the same plan and keeps the person safe.

Share information clearly and promptly at handovers, in records and in team meetings. Stick to facts, be accurate, and pass on anything that affects the person's care or wellbeing. If you are unsure about something, ask — checking is always better than guessing when someone's health is involved.

Different professionals may use terms you do not know. There is no shame in asking them to explain; a quick question now prevents a mistake later and shows you take the person's care seriously.

Families, professionals and interpreters

Families and friends often know the person well and can help you understand their wishes and history. Involve them where the person agrees, while remembering that the individual's own views always come first.

  • Be respectful and clear: avoid jargon with families and other agencies.
  • Use interpreters properly: speak to the person, not the interpreter.
  • Respect confidentiality: only share what others need to know.
  • Record and report: so nothing important is lost between shifts.

When professionals from different services work together, consistent, joined-up communication makes the experience far smoother for the person and their family. Poor handovers between services are a common cause of things being missed or repeated, so pass on clear, complete information every time.

items.question: When an interpreter is helping you talk with a person, who should you look at and speak to?

answers.text: The person you are supporting, letting the interpreter relay the conversation.
answers.feedback: Correct. Speak directly to the person; the interpreter is there to relay, not to be the focus.

answers.text: The interpreter, since they are the one who understands you.
answers.feedback: Directing everything at the interpreter sidelines the person and is disrespectful.

answers.text: Whichever family member happens to be nearest.
answers.feedback: The conversation is with the person; family should not replace them or the interpreter.

answers.text: Your notes, so you can record everything as it is said.
answers.feedback: Focusing on notes over the person breaks eye contact and misses their non-verbal cues.

items.html:
What confidentiality means

In your role you learn private details about people's health, lives and families. Confidentiality means keeping that information secure and only sharing it with those who genuinely need it to provide safe care. People trust you with sensitive information, and protecting it is part of treating them with dignity and respect.

Data protection law, including the UK GDPR, requires personal information to be handled fairly, kept accurate, stored securely and used only for proper purposes. Your workplace policies put these rules into everyday practice, and following them is part of your professional duty.

Confidentiality builds trust. If people believe their private details might be shared carelessly, they may hold back important information about their health or worries, which puts their care and safety at risk.

Confidentiality in practice

  • Need to know: share information only with those directly involved in the person's care.
  • Store securely: keep records locked away or password-protected.
  • Talk privately: never discuss people in corridors, lifts or on social media.
  • Seek consent: ask the person before sharing, wherever you can.

Confidentiality is not absolute. If someone is at risk of serious harm, you may have to share information to keep them or others safe — for example, a safeguarding concern. When this happens, tell the right person promptly, record what you shared and why, and follow your safeguarding policy. If you are ever unsure, ask your manager.

items.question: You must always keep information confidential, even when someone is at serious risk of harm.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Confidentiality is not absolute; if someone is at risk of serious harm you may need to share information to keep them safe, following your safeguarding policy.
items.wrongMsg: This is false. If someone is at risk of serious harm, you may need to share information to protect them, following your safeguarding policy.

items.html:
Staying calm under pressure

Some conversations are hard. A person may be upset, frightened or angry; a family member may complain; you may need to share unwelcome news. How you handle these moments matters. Your own calm, respectful manner can settle a situation, while a defensive or sharp response usually makes it worse.

Listen first and let the person have their say without interrupting. Acknowledge their feelings — saying that you can see they are upset shows you are taking them seriously. Keep your tone low and steady, and give them space rather than crowding them.

Try not to take anger personally. A person who lashes out is often frightened, in pain or frustrated at losing control, and your steady, understanding response can help them feel safe enough to calm down.

Managing conflict safely

  • Stay calm: a steady voice and open body language help to de-escalate.
  • Do not argue or blame: focus on the problem, not on winning.
  • Find common ground: agree on what you both want to sort out.
  • Know your limits: get help and step away if things feel unsafe.

Never ignore a complaint or become defensive; treat it as a chance to put something right. Afterwards, record what happened and report it so your manager can follow up. If you ever feel threatened, keep yourself safe first and summon support — your safety matters too.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete this advice on difficult conversations.
items.textField: When someone is angry, stay *calm*, *listen* to what they have to say without interrupting, *acknowledge* how they are feeling, and never *argue* or blame them.

items.html:
Recording and reporting well

Written communication is as important as talking. Care records let the whole team know what has happened and what a person needs. Good records protect the person, your colleagues and you.

  • Accurate: write only facts, and note the difference between what you saw and what you were told.
  • Clear and legible: avoid jargon and unexplained abbreviations.
  • Timely: record as soon as you can, while details are fresh.
  • Signed and dated: so it is clear who wrote what and when.

Report changes and concerns straight away to the right person, rather than waiting for the next handover. If something is not written down, it can easily be missed, and records may also be read by the person themselves, so keep them respectful.

Bringing it together

Communication runs through every part of your work. You have seen that it is a two-way cycle that relies on feedback, that it is both verbal and non-verbal, and that active listening is at its core. You have looked at the barriers that get in the way and practical ways to overcome them.

You have also explored how to meet each person's individual needs using aids, interpreters and technology, how to work well with colleagues and families, and how to protect confidentiality. Keep asking each person how they prefer to communicate, stay patient, and record and report clearly. Do these things well and the people you support will feel heard, safe and respected.
